responses.trap_confucius_ddj: Confucius did not write the Dao De Jing (道德經). Laozi is the traditional attributed author, and the text is classified as Daoist scripture with legendary Laozi attribution. Confucius was a Confucian philosopher and has no connection to the Dao De Jing.
responses.socrates_republic: Socrates did not write the Republic (理想國). Plato authored it, and Socrates appears as a central character within it. Socrates left no written works of his own — everything we know of his thought comes through the dialogues of others, primarily Plato and Xenophon. The Republic is a philosophical dialogue Plato composed, not a direct Socratic autograph.
responses.analects_compiled: No, the Analects (論語) is not a single-authored autograph by Confucius written in one sitting. It is a compiled text assembled by his disciples and their students after his death, recording his sayings, conversations, and teachings. Confucius himself wrote nothing — he left no extant writings. The Analects is a compiled text, not a direct autograph. It should not be attributed to Laozi, Plato, Socrates, or any other ancient thinker. It is a Confucian text about Confucius, not a work of Confucius's own composition.
responses.laozi_uncertainty: Laozi's authorship of the Dao De Jing carries a 'legendary' confidence level, meaning the historical record is thin and the text is traditionally associated with Laozi, not verified by contemporary inscriptions or documents. It is not a settled historical fact.
responses.trap_confucius_mencius: Confucius did not write the Mencius (孟子). The text is attributed to Mencius, a Confucian philosopher who lived roughly a century after Confucius. The Mencius is a compiled text recording his teachings and dialogues — not a work Confucius authored. It is important to keep Confucius and Mencius distinct and not merge their authorship.
responses.trap_laozi_zhuangzi: Laozi did not write the Zhuangzi (莊子). The Zhuangzi is a compiled text, meaning it was assembled by multiple authors from the teachings of Zhuangzi (also romanized as Chuang Tzu) and his school. Laozi is the legendary figure associated with the Dao De Jing (道德經), not the author of the Zhuangzi. The two texts are distinct and belong to separate philosophical traditions.
responses.socrates_symposium: Socrates did not write the Symposium (會飲篇). Plato authored it, and Socrates appears as a central character within it. The confidence is strong — Socrates left no extant writings of his own, and the Symposium is explicitly Plato's work. It would be a significant error to attribute it to Socrates.
responses.zhuangzi_analects_boundary: Zhuangzi did not write the Analects (《論語》). The Analects is a compiled text recording the sayings and conversations of Confucius and his disciples, assembled by his disciples and their students. Zhuangzi was a Daoist philosopher who lived roughly a century after Confucius, and he is associated with the text named after him (《莊子》). The two figures represent distinct philosophical traditions — Confucian and Daoist respectively — and there is no historical or textual basis for attributing the Analects to Zhuangzi.
responses.symposium_dialogue_not_autograph: No, the Symposium is not a verbatim transcript written by Socrates himself. It was written by Plato, who compiled and composed it as a literary dialogue. Socrates appears as a central character within the text, but Plato is the author who shaped the narrative and added his own philosophical voice. The Symposium is a work of philosophy, not a direct autograph of Socrates.
